Learn SQL

Home · Real-World Use Cases
Home Use Cases Games Projects

15 Production Use Cases

Real-world SQL patterns used in production databases at scale. Each card includes a working query example and key implementation details for PostgreSQL.

01 / ANALYTICS

Analytics Dashboard Queries

Product teams, data analysts
SELECT
  DATE_TRUNC('month', created_at) AS month,
  COUNT(*) AS signups,
  COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE plan = 'pro') AS pro_signups,
  ROUND(
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE plan = 'pro') * 100.0 / COUNT(*), 1
  ) AS pro_pct
FROM users
WHERE created_at >= NOW() - INTERVAL '12 months'
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 1;
  • DATE_TRUNC for time-based grouping (month, week, day)
  • FILTER clause for conditional aggregation without CASE
  • Rolling windows with generate_series for filling date gaps
  • Materialized views for pre-computed dashboard data
DATE_TRUNC FILTER Aggregation
02 / REPORTING

Reporting Pipelines

Business intelligence, finance
WITH monthly_revenue AS (
  SELECT
    DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS month,
    SUM(total) AS revenue
  FROM orders
  WHERE status = 'completed'
  GROUP BY 1
),
with_growth AS (
  SELECT
    month,
    revenue,
    LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month) AS prev_month,
    ROUND(
      (revenue - LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month))
      / LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month) * 100, 1
    ) AS growth_pct
  FROM monthly_revenue
)
SELECT * FROM with_growth ORDER BY month DESC;
  • CTEs (WITH clauses) for readable multi-step computations
  • LAG window function for period-over-period comparisons
  • Revenue, churn, MRR, and cohort retention calculations
  • Scheduled reports via pg_cron or application-level scheduling
CTE LAG Window Functions
03 / SEARCH

Full-Text Search

Content platforms, documentation sites
-- Create a search index
ALTER TABLE articles ADD COLUMN search_vector tsvector
  GENERATED ALWAYS AS (
    setweight(to_tsvector('english', coalesce(title, '')), 'A') ||
    setweight(to_tsvector('english', coalesce(body, '')), 'B')
  ) STORED;

CREATE INDEX idx_articles_search ON articles USING GIN(search_vector);

-- Search with ranking
SELECT title, ts_rank(search_vector, query) AS rank
FROM articles, to_tsquery('english', 'election & results') AS query
WHERE search_vector @@ query
ORDER BY rank DESC
LIMIT 20;
  • Generated tsvector column with weighted fields (title=A, body=B)
  • GIN index for fast full-text search on millions of rows
  • ts_rank for relevance scoring with customizable weights
  • ts_headline for highlighted search result snippets
tsvector GIN Index ts_rank
04 / TIME SERIES

Time Series Analysis

IoT, monitoring, financial data
-- Fill date gaps with generate_series
SELECT
  d.date,
  COALESCE(m.count, 0) AS pageviews,
  AVG(COALESCE(m.count, 0)) OVER (
    ORDER BY d.date
    ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
  ) AS rolling_7day_avg
FROM generate_series(
  '2024-01-01'::date,
  CURRENT_DATE,
  '1 day'::interval
) AS d(date)
LEFT JOIN daily_metrics m ON m.date = d.date
ORDER BY d.date;
  • generate_series fills gaps in time series data
  • Rolling averages with ROWS BETWEEN window frames
  • BRIN indexes for time-ordered append-only tables
  • Table partitioning by month/year for large time series
generate_series Window Frames BRIN Index
05 / RECURSIVE

Recursive Hierarchies

Org charts, comment threads, categories
-- Find all nested comments for an article
WITH RECURSIVE comment_tree AS (
  -- Base case: top-level comments
  SELECT id, parent_id, body, author, 1 AS depth,
         ARRAY[id] AS path
  FROM comments
  WHERE article_id = 42 AND parent_id IS NULL

  UNION ALL

  -- Recursive case: replies
  SELECT c.id, c.parent_id, c.body, c.author, ct.depth + 1,
         ct.path || c.id
  FROM comments c
  JOIN comment_tree ct ON c.parent_id = ct.id
)
SELECT * FROM comment_tree ORDER BY path;
  • Recursive CTEs for tree traversal (comments, org charts, categories)
  • Path array for maintaining sort order across nested levels
  • Depth column for indentation in UI rendering
  • Cycle detection with CYCLE clause (PostgreSQL 14+)
Recursive CTE Tree Traversal ARRAY path
06 / PIVOT

Pivot Tables & Crosstabs

Reporting, spreadsheet-style views
-- Pivot monthly sales by product category
SELECT
  product_category,
  SUM(amount) FILTER (WHERE month = 1) AS jan,
  SUM(amount) FILTER (WHERE month = 2) AS feb,
  SUM(amount) FILTER (WHERE month = 3) AS mar,
  SUM(amount) FILTER (WHERE month = 4) AS apr,
  SUM(amount) FILTER (WHERE month = 5) AS may,
  SUM(amount) FILTER (WHERE month = 6) AS jun
FROM (
  SELECT category AS product_category,
         EXTRACT(MONTH FROM sale_date) AS month,
         total AS amount
  FROM sales
  WHERE sale_date >= '2024-01-01'
) sub
GROUP BY product_category
ORDER BY product_category;
  • FILTER clause turns rows into columns (manual pivot)
  • crosstab() from tablefunc extension for dynamic pivots
  • Useful for monthly breakdowns, category comparisons, heat maps
  • Alternative: JSON aggregation for flexible column output
FILTER Pivot crosstab
07 / MIGRATION

Data Migration Scripts

Schema evolution, data ops
-- Safe column rename with zero downtime
BEGIN;
  -- 1. Add new column
  ALTER TABLE articles ADD COLUMN published_at TIMESTAMPTZ;

  -- 2. Backfill from old column
  UPDATE articles SET published_at = pub_date;

  -- 3. Add NOT NULL constraint after backfill
  ALTER TABLE articles ALTER COLUMN published_at SET NOT NULL;

  -- 4. Create index concurrently (outside transaction in practice)
  -- CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_articles_published ON articles(published_at);

  -- 5. Drop old column (after app code is updated)
  -- ALTER TABLE articles DROP COLUMN pub_date;
COMMIT;
  • Transactional DDL for safe schema changes
  • CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY to avoid locking production tables
  • Backfill patterns for large tables (batch updates)
  • Blue-green deployment: new column first, then update code, then drop old
DDL CONCURRENTLY Zero Downtime
08 / AUDIT

Audit Logging with Triggers

Compliance, healthcare, finance
-- Generic audit trigger function
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION audit_trigger()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
BEGIN
  INSERT INTO audit_log (
    table_name, row_id, action,
    old_data, new_data, changed_by, changed_at
  ) VALUES (
    TG_TABLE_NAME,
    COALESCE(NEW.id, OLD.id),
    TG_OP,
    CASE WHEN TG_OP IN ('UPDATE','DELETE') THEN row_to_json(OLD) END,
    CASE WHEN TG_OP IN ('INSERT','UPDATE') THEN row_to_json(NEW) END,
    current_setting('app.current_user', true),
    NOW()
  );
  RETURN COALESCE(NEW, OLD);
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE TRIGGER articles_audit
AFTER INSERT OR UPDATE OR DELETE ON articles
FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION audit_trigger();
  • Generic trigger function works on any table without modification
  • JSONB storage for old/new row data preserves schema changes
  • current_setting for passing application-level user context
  • Append-only audit table with partitioning for retention policies
Triggers row_to_json plpgsql
09 / SECURITY

Multi-Tenant Row-Level Security

SaaS platforms, enterprise apps
-- Enable RLS on the articles table
ALTER TABLE articles ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

-- Tenants can only see their own data
CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation ON articles
  USING (tenant_id = current_setting('app.tenant_id')::int);

-- Admins can see all data
CREATE POLICY admin_access ON articles
  USING (current_setting('app.role') = 'admin');

-- Set tenant context per request
SET app.tenant_id = '42';
SET app.role = 'user';

-- This query automatically filters by tenant_id = 42
SELECT * FROM articles;  -- Only sees tenant 42's data
  • Row-Level Security (RLS) enforces data isolation at the database level
  • Policies are invisible to application code (automatic filtering)
  • Session variables (SET) pass tenant context from the application
  • Multiple policies for different roles (tenant, admin, support)
RLS Policies Multi-tenant
10 / GEO

Geospatial Queries with PostGIS

Mapping, delivery, real estate
-- Find all restaurants within 2 miles of a location
SELECT name, address,
  ST_Distance(
    location::geography,
    ST_MakePoint(-73.9857, 40.7484)::geography
  ) / 1609.34 AS miles
FROM restaurants
WHERE ST_DWithin(
  location::geography,
  ST_MakePoint(-73.9857, 40.7484)::geography,
  3218.69  -- 2 miles in meters
)
ORDER BY miles
LIMIT 20;
  • PostGIS extension for geographic data types and spatial functions
  • ST_DWithin for efficient proximity searches with spatial indexes
  • GiST indexes on geometry/geography columns for fast spatial queries
  • Polygon containment, route optimization, and boundary analysis
PostGIS ST_DWithin GiST Index
11 / JSON

JSON Document Queries

Flexible schemas, API responses
-- Query into JSONB data
SELECT
  metadata->>'title' AS title,
  metadata->'author'->>'name' AS author,
  (metadata->>'view_count')::int AS views
FROM articles
WHERE metadata @> '{"status": "published"}'
  AND metadata->'tags' ? 'breaking'
ORDER BY (metadata->>'view_count')::int DESC;

-- GIN index for fast JSONB containment queries
CREATE INDEX idx_articles_metadata ON articles
  USING GIN(metadata jsonb_path_ops);
  • JSONB operators: -> (object), ->> (text), @> (containment), ? (key exists)
  • GIN indexes with jsonb_path_ops for 3x faster containment queries
  • jsonb_agg and jsonb_object_agg for building JSON from relational data
  • Hybrid approach: relational columns for indexed fields, JSONB for flexible data
JSONB GIN Index jsonb_path_ops
12 / VIEWS

Materialized Views

Dashboards, reporting, caching
-- Create a materialized view for dashboard stats
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW dashboard_stats AS
SELECT
  DATE_TRUNC('day', created_at) AS day,
  COUNT(*) AS total_articles,
  COUNT(DISTINCT author_id) AS active_authors,
  AVG(word_count) AS avg_word_count,
  SUM(view_count) AS total_views
FROM articles
WHERE created_at >= NOW() - INTERVAL '90 days'
GROUP BY 1
WITH DATA;

-- Unique index for concurrent refresh
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ON dashboard_stats(day);

-- Refresh without locking reads
REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY dashboard_stats;
  • Pre-computed query results stored as a table for fast reads
  • CONCURRENTLY refresh avoids locking during updates
  • Unique index required for concurrent refresh
  • Schedule refreshes with pg_cron for automatic updates
Materialized View CONCURRENTLY pg_cron
13 / ETL

ETL Patterns

Data warehousing, data engineering
-- Upsert pattern for incremental loads
INSERT INTO dim_users (id, name, email, updated_at)
SELECT id, name, email, NOW()
FROM staging_users
ON CONFLICT (id) DO UPDATE SET
  name = EXCLUDED.name,
  email = EXCLUDED.email,
  updated_at = NOW()
WHERE dim_users.name != EXCLUDED.name
   OR dim_users.email != EXCLUDED.email;

-- Track what changed (SCD Type 2)
INSERT INTO dim_users_history (user_id, name, email, valid_from, valid_to)
SELECT id, name, email, updated_at, NULL
FROM staging_users s
WHERE NOT EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 FROM dim_users d
  WHERE d.id = s.id AND d.name = s.name AND d.email = s.email
);
  • ON CONFLICT (upsert) for idempotent incremental loads
  • EXCLUDED pseudo-table references the incoming row in conflict resolution
  • Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCD Type 2) for historical tracking
  • COPY command for bulk loading CSV/TSV data at 100K+ rows/second
Upsert SCD COPY
14 / INFRA

Connection Pooling

High-traffic applications
-- PgBouncer configuration (pgbouncer.ini)
-- [databases]
-- mydb = host=localhost port=5432 dbname=mydb
--
-- [pgbouncer]
-- pool_mode = transaction
-- max_client_conn = 1000
-- default_pool_size = 25
-- min_pool_size = 5

-- Monitor active connections
SELECT
  datname,
  count(*) AS total,
  count(*) FILTER (WHERE state = 'active') AS active,
  count(*) FILTER (WHERE state = 'idle') AS idle,
  count(*) FILTER (WHERE wait_event IS NOT NULL) AS waiting
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE datname = 'mydb'
GROUP BY datname;
  • PgBouncer reduces connection overhead (fork cost: ~10ms per connection)
  • Transaction pooling mode for serverless/high-concurrency workloads
  • pg_stat_activity for monitoring connection states and finding leaks
  • Idle connection timeout to prevent connection exhaustion
PgBouncer Pooling pg_stat_activity
15 / PERF

Performance Profiling

DBAs, backend engineers
-- Find the slowest queries
SELECT
  calls,
  ROUND(total_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS total_ms,
  ROUND(mean_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS avg_ms,
  ROUND(stddev_exec_time::numeric, 2) AS stddev_ms,
  rows,
  LEFT(query, 80) AS query_preview
FROM pg_stat_statements
ORDER BY total_exec_time DESC
LIMIT 20;

-- Analyze a specific query
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT)
SELECT a.title, u.name
FROM articles a
JOIN users u ON a.author_id = u.id
WHERE a.status = 'published'
  AND a.pub_date > '2024-01-01'
ORDER BY a.pub_date DESC
LIMIT 50;
  • pg_stat_statements tracks cumulative query performance
  • EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) shows actual plan with I/O details
  • Look for Seq Scan on large tables, Nested Loop on unindexed JOINs
  • auto_explain logs slow query plans automatically for debugging
pg_stat_statements EXPLAIN ANALYZE BUFFERS
